reasoning:
Based on recent events in early 2025, Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin have maintained a high cadence of direct communication, holding lengthy phone calls roughly once a month (February 12 and March 18) to negotiate a resolution to the Ukraine war. Diplomacy has sharply escalated recently, with U.S. envoy Steve Witkoff meeting Putin in Moscow on April 25, prompting Trump to announce they are "very close to a deal."

A critical factor for this specific 34-day time window (May 12 – June 15) is Trump's scheduled trip to the Middle East (Saudi Arabia, Qatar, UAE) from May 13 to May 16. Initially, there was widespread speculation that Trump and Putin would hold an in-person summit in Saudi Arabia during this trip. However, on May 5, the Kremlin officially stated that Putin has no immediate plans to travel to the Middle East, ruling out this venue for a face-to-face meeting. At the same time, the Kremlin reaffirmed that leader-to-leader contact remains "necessary."

Because the in-person meeting in mid-May has fallen through, a phone call during or immediately following Trump's Middle East trip serves as the most natural and highly probable substitute to finalize the terms of the "very close" ceasefire deal, especially since Saudi Crown Prince MBS has been a key mediator. Although some U.S. advisers reportedly want Trump to withhold a call until a formal ceasefire is agreed upon, Trump's eagerness to broker a deal and his history of prioritizing direct leader engagement strongly suggest he will be on the phone with Putin within this window. The timing aligns perfectly.
